entries.intro: The well near {subject_focus_the} needs rebuilding and nobody can agree on who should pay for it. It's been three meetings and counting.
details: The north side of the village uses it most, but the south side owns the land it sits on. Both sides think the other should foot the bill.
Meanwhile the well wall is crumbling. Another season and we'll be fishing stones out of our drinking water.
The mason offered to do it at cost if they'd just decide. They're still deciding.
reactions: It's not really about the well. It's about the road dispute from two years ago that never got resolved. The well is just the latest battlefield.
I told them I'd pay a share myself if it'd break the deadlock. Both sides said they appreciated the gesture and went right back to arguing.

entries.intro: A family of refugees showed up at {subject_focus_the} last week. Eight people, two carts, everything they own packed into bundles.
details: They say their village was abandoned after the river changed course. No water, no crops, no choice but to move.
The elders offered them the empty plot near the mill, but some of the village folk aren't happy about sharing resources with strangers.
The father is a skilled tanner. He's already started helping at the leather shop without being asked.
reactions: We've got room and we've got food. Turning people away when they've lost everything isn't who we are.
I understand the worry. Resources are tight enough without eight more mouths. But tight and impossible are different things.

statCheck.pass: I talked to the father quietly. He mentioned their village wasn't just abandoned: they were the last to leave. Others left months ago, heading the same direction. Something is pushing people out of the lowlands.
statCheck.fail: They are tired and guarded, that family. I asked a few questions myself and got the basics, nothing more. They keep the details close.

entries.intro: The old meeting hall near {subject_focus_the} burned down last month, and rebuilding it has turned into the biggest project this village has seen in years.
details: Everyone's contributing something: timber, labor, nails. Even the children are carrying water for the workers.
The new design is bigger than the old one. Someone suggested adding a second floor for storage, and somehow it stuck.
The carpenter and the mason haven't agreed on a single thing yet, but the building keeps going up anyway.
reactions: It's the most united I've seen this village in a decade. Nothing brings people together like a common project.
I've been cooking for the work crews. It's not much, but someone has to keep these people fed.

entries.intro: The baker near {subject_focus_the} has been leaving bread on doorsteps for the families who can't afford it. She won't admit to it, but everybody knows.
details: She bakes an extra batch every evening and it's gone by morning. Always the same households: the widow, the old soldier, the family with six children.
She uses her own flour for it. Won't take donations. Says it's just surplus that would go stale.
The widow tried to thank her once. She pretended not to know what the woman was talking about.
reactions: In a village this size, a quiet kindness like that holds more weight than people realize. It sets a standard.
The miller has started giving her a discount on flour. His way of contributing without making a fuss about it.

entries.intro: The youth near {subject_focus_the} have been leaving. One or two a season, heading for the cities. The village is getting older every year.
details: Three left this spring alone. The tanner's apprentice, the farrier's daughter, and the best young hunter we had.
They write letters home sometimes. The city work pays better, but the letters sound tired.
The village council talked about offering land grants to keep young people here, but there's not enough good land left to offer.
reactions: I don't blame them for wanting more. I blame us for not finding a way to give them a reason to stay.
Every skill that walks out the gate is a skill we might not get back. The blacksmith is sixty-two and has no apprentice.

statCheck.pass: I spoke with the farrier's daughter before she left. She said it wasn't the money: it was the feeling that nothing here would ever change. She wanted to matter somewhere. That hit harder than I expected.
statCheck.fail: Everyone just shrugs when you bring it up. Says it's always been this way. Maybe it has.
